title: End of module: Programming Paradigms: OOP and Functional

questions.text: Which programming paradigm bundles an object's data together with the functions (methods) that operate on that data?
choices: None of them — data and functions are always kept separate
questions.explanation: Object-oriented programming groups data (attributes) and behaviour (methods) together inside a class, so each object carries both its state and the operations on that state.

questions.text: In Python, which special method is called automatically to set up a new object's attributes when the object is created?
questions.explanation: `__init__` is the constructor: Python creates a new (empty) object, then calls `__init__(self, ...)` on it to attach its initial attributes.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly describe **encapsulation**?
choices: Bundling an object's data together with the methods that operate on it
Allowing a subclass to automatically reuse a superclass's methods
Restricting direct access to an object's internal data and exposing controlled methods instead
The same method call producing different behaviour depending on the object's class
questions.explanation: Encapsulation is bundling data with its methods and controlling access to that data (e.g. `_balance` changed only via `deposit`/`withdraw`). Option 1 describes inheritance and option 3 describes polymorphism, not encapsulation.

questions.text:
Given `def factorial(n):
    if n == 0:
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n - 1)`, what does `factorial(5)` evaluate to?

questions.explanation: The sequence from the two base cases is $F(0)=0, F(1)=1, F(2)=1, F(3)=2, F(4)=3, F(5)=5, F(6)=8$ — each term is the sum of the previous two, so $F(6) = F(5) + F(4) = 5 + 3 = 8$.

questions.text: Using the same `factorial` function as above, how many total calls to `factorial` (including the very first call) happen when evaluating `factorial(6)`?
questions.explanation: Each recursive case reduces `n` by exactly 1 until the base case `n == 0`, so evaluating `factorial(6)` makes one call for each of `n = 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0` — that is $6 + 1 = 7$ calls in total.
`map` applies the lambda to every element: $1 \times 2 = 2$, $2 \times 2 = 4$, $3 \times 2 = 6$, giving `[2, 4, 6]`.
`filter` keeps only elements for which the lambda returns `True`: $1>2$ and $2>2$ are false, $3>2$ and $4>2$ are true, so the surviving elements are `[3, 4]`.

questions.text: What single term describes a function that has no side effects and always returns the same output for the same input?
questions.explanation: This describes a **pure function** — its result depends only on its arguments, and calling it never changes anything outside itself.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about recursion are true?
choices: Every correct recursive function needs at least one base case that stops further recursive calls
Each recursive call should move strictly closer to a base case
A recursive function is only allowed to call itself once inside its body
Recursion can be a natural alternative to iteration for problems with self-similar substructure
questions.explanation: A base case is essential to stop the recursion, and each call must progress toward it or the function never terminates. Option 2 is false: `fibonacci` above calls itself **twice** in its recursive case (`fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)`), which is perfectly valid.

choices: It raises an error because `Bird` has no `Eagle` or `Penguin` methods
questions.explanation: Each object's own class is used to look up `move`: the plain `Bird` has no override so it uses `Bird.move` (`"walks"`); `Eagle` and `Penguin` each override `move` with their own version (`"flies"`, `"swims"`). This is polymorphism — the same call `b.move()` behaves differently per object.
